Environment: Spine-Leaf Data Center running VXLAN with BGP EVPN control plane.
Business Context: A new VLAN segment is deployed for virtualization storage traffic. Dynamic replication of multicast MAC frames fails between VTEPs, dropping BUM storage clustering heartbeats.
Network Diagram (ASCII):
[Leaf-1 VTEP] <--- BGP EVPN spine ---> [Leaf-2 VTEP]
 VTEP-1: 10.1.1.1                        VTEP-2: 10.1.1.2
Problem Statement: Route Target import/export configuration mismatch for EVPN Route Type 3 (Inclusive Multicast Ethernet Tag - IMET) prevents Leaves from building dynamic ingress replication lists, dropping BUM traffic.
Symptoms: Multicast storage clustering traffic does not cross leaves. Broadcast ARP resolutions fail to resolve across leaves.
Error Messages: BGP-3-EVPN_IMET_DROP: Received Route Type-3 from 10.1.1.1 but dropped: no matching import Route Target
Logs: 2026-06-27T12:05:01Z Leaf-2 %BGP-5-EVPN_RT3: Received IMET Type-3 route from 10.1.1.1 but skipped importing: RT list [65000:20200] does not match local import targets
Monitoring Alerts: EVPN-BUM-Replication-Failed: Dynamic ingress replication list is empty on Leaf-2. Severity: Critical.
Root Cause: Leaf-1 configured export Route Target as 65000:20200, while Leaf-2 had its import Route Target configured as 65000:20300 under the EVPN VRF/EVPN instance context, causing the IMET (Type 3) route to be discarded.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. View EVPN routing database on Leaf-2 using 'show bgp l2vpn evpn route-type 3'.
2. Inspect VRF configuration and route-target imports.
3. Align import route-target values.
Commands Executed:
show bgp l2vpn evpn route-type 3
show run vrf
Resolution: Corrected the import Route Target on Leaf-2 under the EVPN instance to match 65000:20200.
Validation Steps: Verify remote ingress replication table with 'show nve vxlan' and check if BUM replication works.
Rollback Procedure:
Leaf-2(config)# vrf context storage
Leaf-2(config-vrf)# address-family ipv4 unicast
Leaf-2(config-vrf-af)# no route-target import 65000:20200
Leaf-2(config-vrf-af)# route-target import 65000:20300
Preventive Measures: Auto-derive route-targets using the BGP AS and VNI number.
Best Practices: Deploy VXLAN configurations using automated configuration templates.
Security Considerations: Filter EVPN route exchanges at the spines using prefix-lists.
Automation Opportunity: Ansible playbook deploying leaf EVPN variables.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Verify EVPN route-target parameters on Leaf-1 and Leaf-2. Identify why Leaf-2 ignores Leaf-1 route announcements.
Interview Questions: What is the function of EVPN Route Type 3? How does it differ from Route Type 2?

Business Context: A remote branch reports frequent short dropouts of VoIP and video calls. The monitoring logs show the SD-WAN data plane tunnel flaps continuously, dropping calls.
Problem Statement: BFD hello interval mismatch on the SD-WAN tunnel interface causes the link to be declared dead during slight traffic bursts, triggering tunnel flap.
Symptoms: Call quality drops. Tunnel status flaps between UP and DOWN. Traffic routes back and forth.
Error Messages: %SDWAN-3-BFD_FLAP: BFD session on Tunnel0 to peer 198.51.100.1 flapped: Timeout expired
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:10:02Z Branch-Edge %BFD-5-NBRCHANGE: BFD neighbor 198.51.100.1 is DOWN: Timeout
2026-06-27T12:10:05Z Branch-Edge %SDWAN-5-TUNNEL_FLAP: Tunnel0 state changed to UP
Monitoring Alerts: SDWAN-Tunnel-Flapping: BFD session on Tunnel0 is unstable. Severity: Major.
Root Cause: The branch router BFD hello multiplier was set to 3 with a hello interval of 100ms, while the HQ gateway was set to a hello interval of 1000ms. Since the branch expected a hello every 100ms, it declared the tunnel dead before the HQ hello arrived.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. View BFD session details with 'show bfd neighbors'.
2. Compare hello interval settings on both endpoints.
3. Align BFD hello parameters using vManage templates.
Commands Executed:
show bfd neighbors
show run interface Tunnel0
Expected Output: BFD hello interval: 1000ms, Multiplier: 3 (matching on both ends)
Resolution: Updated the branch BFD template on vManage to set the hello interval to 1000ms, matching the HQ gateway.
Validation Steps: Verify tunnel stability using 'show bfd neighbors' and check packet drop counts.
Preventive Measures: Enforce uniform BFD template policies across all SD-WAN templates.
Best Practices: Use 1000ms hello and multiplier of 3 for standard WAN interfaces to prevent false positive triggers during micro-congestion.
Security Considerations: Enable BFD authentication on the overlay connections.
Automation Opportunity: API script validating BFD timers across all SD-WAN profiles.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Diagnose SD-WAN BFD session flapping. Sync hello timers to 1000ms on both peers.
Interview Questions: What is BFD and how does it assist routing protocol convergence? Why do mismatched timers cause flaps?

Environment: Cisco Nexus Leaf switches running Virtual Port Channel (vPC).
Business Context: A newly added server rack fails to connect to the datacenter network. The link ports on Nexus switches are suspended, blocking connection.
Network Diagram (ASCII):
[SW-Core-01] <==== 802.1Q vPC Peer-link ====> [SW-Core-02]
               \                              /
                \                            /
                 +==== vPC Member Link Po10 =+
                              |
                        [Server Host]
Problem Statement: vPC Type-1 inconsistency error due to mismatched MTU configuration on the member ports suspends the vPC link.
Symptoms: Port channel status shows suspended. Server cannot ping default gateway. Nexus syslog shows vPC consistency check failure.
Error Messages: %NOHMS-2-VPC_MUTUAL_CON_FAIL: vPC Type-1 inconsistency: MTU mismatch for port-channel 10 (Local: 9000, Peer: 1500)
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:15:02Z N7K-1 %VPC-2-PEER_KEEP_ALIVE: vPC peer keep-alive is up
2026-06-27T12:15:05Z N7K-1 %VPC-2-PORT_CH_SUSPENDED: Port-channel 10 suspended on peer switch due to Type-1 consistency mismatch
Monitoring Alerts: vPC-Port-Suspended: Port-channel 10 suspended due to Type-1 mismatch. Severity: Major.
Root Cause: Nexus Switch 1 had MTU configured as 9000 (Jumbo frames) on Port-channel 10, whereas Switch 2 had it configured as 1500. MTU is a Type-1 consistency parameter, so mismatched values force the vPC engine to suspend the port-channel.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. View vPC consistency status with 'show vpc consistency-parameters interface po10'.
2. Compare configuration of Port-channel 10 on both switches.
3. Align interface MTU parameters.
Commands Executed:
show vpc consistency-parameters interface po10
show interface po10
Expected Output: MTU Type-1 consistency checks: Success (9000 bytes on both switches)
Resolution: Updated Switch 2's Port-channel 10 configuration to run at MTU 9000, matching Switch 1.
Validation Steps: Verify using 'show vpc' and check if member interfaces are up (P state).
Preventive Measures: Validate vPC consistency parameters before adding physical ports to dynamic port channels.
Best Practices: Always apply MTU configurations globally using policies on Nexus devices to maintain uniformity.
Security Considerations: Protect vPC peer-keepalive links from external spoofing using VRFs.
Automation Opportunity: Python script checking consistency variables via Cisco NX-API.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Diagnose Nexus vPC Type-1 consistency error. Align MTU on Port-channel 10 to 9000.
Interview Questions: What is a vPC Type-1 inconsistency and what are some examples of parameters checked? What happens to the secondary switch ports when a Type-1 check fails?

Environment: Enterprise Core network using OSPF routing.
Business Context: Following a router installation, routes across the enterprise began flapping rapidly. Critical applications experienced intermittent dropouts every few seconds.
Problem Statement: Duplicate OSPF Router ID configured on R1 and R3 in Area 0 causes SPF database conflict and continuous route flaps.
Symptoms: Routes are added and withdrawn continuously. OSPF processor utilization reaches 100%. Pings show packet drops.
Error Messages: %OSPF-4-DUP_RTRID: Duplicate Router ID 10.0.0.1 discovered on neighbor 10.1.1.3
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:20:01Z R1 %OSPF-4-DUP_RTRID: OSPF neighbor 10.1.1.3 has duplicate Router ID 10.0.0.1
2026-06-27T12:20:04Z R1 %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process 1, Nbr 10.0.0.1 on Gi0/1 from FULL to DOWN
Monitoring Alerts: OSPF-Duplicate-RouterID: Duplicate Router ID 10.0.0.1 detected in Area 0. Severity: Critical.
Root Cause: R1 and R3 were manually configured with the same Router ID (10.0.0.1). When R3 announced its Link State Advertisements (LSAs), R1 saw its own Router ID in the source field but with different routing details, causing it to send poison LSAs and triggering a database synchronization loop.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. Check router-id configuration on all OSPF routers using 'show ip ospf'.
2. Identify the duplicate values.
3. Re-configure a unique router-id on R3.
Commands Executed:
show ip ospf
show ip ospf database
Expected Output: R1 Router ID: 10.0.0.1, R3 Router ID: 10.0.0.3
Resolution: Updated the router-id configuration on R3 under 'router ospf 1' to 'router-id 10.0.0.3' and cleared the OSPF process.
Validation Steps: Verify OSPF database stability using 'show ip ospf database' and monitor interface routes.
Rollback Procedure: None needed (restoring duplicates is not a valid rollback).
Preventive Measures: Implement centralized IPAM (IP Address Management) to track loopback addresses and Router IDs.
Best Practices: Always use the loopback address of the router as its OSPF router-id to prevent collisions.
Security Considerations: Enforce MD5 neighbor authentication to prevent unauthorized OSPF routers from injecting colliding IDs.
Automation Opportunity: Ansible playbook verifying unique router-ids on active configurations.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Identify duplicate OSPF router-id. Change R3's router-id to 10.0.0.3 and clear the process.
Interview Questions: Why does a duplicate OSPF router-id cause route flapping? How does OSPF resolve LSA collisions?

Business Context: The VPN tunnel connecting corporate HQ to staging servers drops traffic exactly every 8 hours under heavy database replication load, disrupting automated backups.
Network Diagram (ASCII):
[HQ-Firewall] <================ IPSec Tunnel (Phase 2 Rekey) ================> [Staging-Gateway]
 PFS Group: DH14                                                                 PFS Group: DH2
Problem Statement: IPSec Tunnel fails during Phase 2 re-keying because of mismatched Diffie-Hellman group settings in the crypto map profile.
Symptoms: Tunnel drops traffic during re-key intervals. Syslog shows proposal rejection during Phase 2 negotiation.
Error Messages: %CRYPTO-4-REKEY_FAIL: IPSec SA rekey with peer 203.0.113.10 failed: No proposal matches
Logs:
2026-06-27T12:25:01Z HQ-FW %CRYPTO-4-RECVD_PKT_INV_SPI: Packet from 203.0.113.10 has invalid SPI during rekey
2026-06-27T12:25:04Z HQ-FW %CRYPTO-5-SESSION_TIMEOUT: Session with 203.0.113.10 expired
Monitoring Alerts: VPN-Rekey-Failed: Rekey failure on staging tunnel. Severity: Major.
Root Cause: HQ Firewall had Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) set to DH Group 14, whereas the staging gateway had PFS set to DH Group 2. While the initial tunnel setup succeeded because of fallback profiles, Phase 2 re-keying enforced DH Group 14, causing the staging gateway to reject the negotiation proposal.
Step-by-Step Troubleshooting:
1. View crypto configuration using 'show run crypto'.
2. Check VPN logs during re-key intervals.
3. Align DH group parameters under staging gateway profiles.
Commands Executed:
show run crypto
show crypto ipsec sa
Expected Output: PFS DH Group 14 configured on both endpoints.
Actual Output: HQ: DH Group 14, Staging: DH Group 2
Resolution: Updated the staging gateway crypto policy to use DH Group 14 for Phase 2 PFS.
Validation Steps: Force tunnel re-keying using 'clear crypto sa' and verify that it remains active.
Rollback Procedure:
Staging-Gateway(config)# crypto map vpn-map 10
Staging-Gateway(config-crypto-map)# set pfs group2
Preventive Measures: Mandate the use of DH Group 14 or higher for all enterprise IPSec connections.
Best Practices: Verify Phase 1 and Phase 2 lifetimes and security groups match before deploying links.
Security Considerations: Avoid low DH groups (1, 2, 5) as they are cryptographically vulnerable.
Automation Opportunity: Ansible playbook implementing standard crypto profile templates across all gateways.
AI Troubleshooting Prompt: Resolve IPSec tunnel re-key failure. Set PFS DH group to 14 on staging gateway.
Interview Questions: Why does a VPN connection work initially but fail during re-keying? What is the function of PFS?
